does: This paper examines AI red teaming through a human-centered lens, identifying critical social and collaborative dimensions that have been overlooked in technical red teaming literature. It surveys psychological and workplace impacts on red teamers themselves, documents how selection biases and personal blindspots shape what vulnerabilities get discovered, and catalogs potential harms to human participants conducting adversarial testing. The work positions red teaming as a sociotechnical practice comparable to data labeling and content moderation, where human factors significantly influence both the quality of testing and worker wellbeing, establishing red teaming as a topic requiring rigorous HCI and CSCW investigation rather than purely technical approaches.
connects: This work builds on established HCI and CSCW research examining the human dimensions of AI development and governance, including studies of data labeling practices, content moderation labor, and algorithmic auditing. It responds to rapid scaling of AI red teaming adoption without corresponding attention to worker experience or systematic study of testing effectiveness. The paper extends frameworks from crowdwork ethics and participatory design that highlight how invisible human labor shapes AI system behavior and safety. It draws on cybersecurity red teaming literature while noting that AI red teaming creates distinct challenges absent from traditional security testing, particularly around psychological exposure to harmful content and the epistemological question of whose perspectives get represented in vulnerability discovery.
enables: This framing opens multiple research directions at the intersection of AI safety and worker welfare. Future work can investigate recruitment and selection biases in red teaming, examining how diverse team composition affects coverage of failure modes and whether current selection practices miss certain classes of harms. It enables studies of red teamer mental health, burnout, and appropriate support structures, drawing methods from content moderation research. The paper points toward fairness audits of red teaming itself—determining equitable compensation, psychological safety, and inclusion practices. It also suggests methodological innovations combining human-centered research with technical testing, potentially establishing red teaming as a legitimate interdisciplinary field rather than a purely engineering concern, with implications for how future AI governance structures this critical practice.
